nativeRange.habitat: Shallow lagoon and seaward reefs in areas of mixed coral, rock, or sand, typically at depths of 2–30 m

diet.notes: Has been documented feeding primarily on benthic algae and detritus in the wild. In captivity, generally advised to offer dried marine seaweed (nori) on a clip at least three times per week, alongside spirulina-based frozen and prepared foods. Meaty foods such as mysis are tolerated occasionally, but a vegetable-dominant diet is widely considered important for long-term coloration and immune health.

avoidWith: fish of similar body shape and color

summary: An Indian Ocean surgeonfish whose juveniles mimic the Eibli Angelfish (Centropyge eibli). Generally considered reef-safe and a useful algae grazer; tends to be highly aggressive toward other tangs.
careNotes:
The Mimic Eibli Tang (Acanthurus tristis) is an Indian Ocean surgeonfish best known for its juvenile mimicry of the Eibli Angelfish (Centropyge eibli) — a trait thought to afford some predator protection on the reef. Juveniles display blue, white, and yellow coloration with striking orange vertical lines and a yellow-to-orange belly that darkens toward a black caudal region. Adults gradually shift toward more uniform brown tones with retained orange highlights near the eye and pectoral fins.

Like all Acanthurus surgeonfish, this species possesses a sharp, blade-like caudal-peduncle spine (scalpel) capable of inflicting cuts on handlers and tankmates — caution is advised when netting or moving the fish. It has been known to be very aggressive toward conspecifics and other tang/surgeonfish species, particularly those with similar body shape, but tends to be peaceful with unrelated fish. Generally advised to keep only one specimen unless the aquarium is exceptionally large.

A well-established reef aquarium with ample live rock for grazing, open swimming space, and strong, well-oxygenated flow is generally advised. Where natural algae is insufficient, regular nori or spirulina-based supplementation helps reduce aggression and prevents Head and Lateral Line Erosion (HLLE), to which this species — like most tangs — is reported to be susceptible when kept in poor water quality or on a low-algae diet.

Source disagreement notes: Adult size is consistent across sources at ~25 cm max (LiveAquaria 10 in / 25.4 cm; FishBase 25.0 cm TL; ReefApp 25 cm). Temperature ranges differ between captive and wild sources — LiveAquaria recommends 72–78°F (22.2–25.6°C) for aquariums, while FishBase reports a wild range of 28–29°C; the captive range used here (24–27°C) reflects a moderate aquarium-hobby average. Minimum tank volume varied substantially: LiveAquaria specifies 125 gal (~473 L); ReefApp specifies 1000 L. Per the cautious-larger rule for active surgeonfish that need ample swimming space, the value used (700 L) is biased toward the larger figure while remaining a common nice-round tank size.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Species page for Mimic Eibli Tang. Provided care level, temperament, water parameters (pH, SG, temperature in F, dKH), maximum size, diet, minimum tank size, and reef-compatibility.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Acanthuridae / Acanthuriformes), adult size (25.0 cm TL), wild temperature range (28–29°C), depth range (2–30 m), native distribution (Indian Ocean from Bay of Bengal to southern Indonesia), and juvenile mimicry of Centropyge eibli.

additional.name: ReefApp Encyclopedia
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (25 cm), minimum tank volume (1000 L — the high end of the source spread), salinity (~1.026 SG), diet (macroalgae + spirulina), and aggression toward similar species.
